Residue Theory

finite interval, say $[-1,1]$, is convergent. For $|x| \geq 1$, we have $\frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} \leq \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}}=$ and since $\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x^{2}} d x$ is convergent, it follows by Proposition 1 that $\int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x \int_{-\infty}^{-1} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x$ are convergent. Thus $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x$ is convergent, and so
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x=\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty} \int_{-R}^{R} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x
$$

Step 2: Set up the contour integral. We will replace $x$ by $z$ and consider the fun $f(z)=\frac{z^{2}}{z^{4}+1}$. The general guideline is to integrate this function over a contour consists partly of the interval $[-R, R]$, so as to recapture the integral $\int_{-R}^{R} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}-4}$ as part of the contour integral on $\gamma_{R}$. Choosing the appropriate contour is obvious in general. For a rational function, such as $\frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1}$, where the denomir does not vanish on the $x$-axis and the degree of the denominator is two more the degree of the numerator, a closed semi-circle $\gamma_{R}$ as in Figure 3 will work. $\gamma_{R}$ consists of the interval $[-R, R]$ followed by the semi-circle $\sigma_{R}$, using the add property of path integrals (Proposition 2(iii), Section 3.2), we write
$$
I_{\gamma_{R}}=\int_{\gamma_{R}} f(z) d z=\int_{[-R, R]} f(z) d z+\int_{\sigma_{R}} f(z) d z=I_{R}+I_{\sigma_{R}}
$$

For $z=x$ in $[-R, R]$, we have $f(z)=f(x)=\frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1}$ and $d z=d x$, and so $\int_{-R}^{R} \frac{x^{2}}{x^{4}+1} d x$, which according to (4) converges to the desired integral as $R$ So, in order to compute the desired integral, we must get a handle on the quantities, $I_{\gamma_{R}}$ and $I_{\sigma_{R}}$, in (5). Our strategy is as follows. In Step 3, we con $I_{\gamma_{R}}$ by the residue theorem; and in Step 4, we show that $\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty} I_{\sigma_{R}}=0$.

Step 3: Compute $I_{\gamma_{R}}$ by the residue theorem. For $R>1$, the function $f(z)=$ has two poles inside $\gamma_{R}$. These are the roots of $z^{4}+1=0$ in the upper halfTo solve $z^{4}=-1$, we write $-1=e^{i \pi}$; then using the formula for the $n$th root Section 1.3, we find the four roots
$$
z_{1}=\frac{1+i}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad z_{2}=\frac{-1+i}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad z_{3}=\frac{-1-i}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad z_{4}=\frac{-1-i}{\sqrt{2}}
$$
(see Figure 3). Since these are simple roots, we have simple poles at $z_{1}$ and the residues there are (Proposition 1(ii), Section 5.1)
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(z_{1}\right)=\left.\frac{z^{2}}{\frac{d}{d z}\left(z^{4}+1\right)}\right|_{z=z_{1}}=\left.\frac{z^{2}}{4 z^{3}}\right|_{z=z_{1}}=\left.\frac{1}{4 z}\right|_{z=z_{1}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4(1+i)}=\frac{1-i}{4 \sqrt{2}}
$$
and similarly
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(z_{2}\right)=\left.\frac{1}{4 z}\right|_{z=z_{2}}=\frac{-1-i}{4 \sqrt{2}} .
$$

So, by the residue theorem, for all $R>1$
$$
I_{\gamma_{R}}=\int_{\gamma_{R}} \frac{z^{2}}{z^{4}+1} d z=2 \pi i\left(\operatorname{Res}\left(z_{1}\right)+\operatorname{Res}\left(z_{2}\right)\right)=2 \pi i \frac{-2 i}{4 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}}
$$
